coredata.dc:title: Viscosity-activated carbon dots for noninvasive disease diagnosis, therapeutic efficacy assessment and anticancer drug screening
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Mingxin
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal levels of intracellular microviscosity have a close relationship with some diseases and pathologies. Therefore, quantitative imaging of viscosity at the cellular and organ levels is beneficial for disease diagnosis, curative effect evaluation, and anticancer drug screening. Herein, we synthesized viscosity-activated orange-red emitting carbon dots (named as LP-CDs) from lignin and p-phenylenediamine using a one-step method, whose fluorescence intensity, lifetime, and quantum yield significantly increase with rising viscosity. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of LP-CDs also has a good linear relationship with the solution viscosity. Taking the advantages of high sensitivity, noninvasiveness, and rapid result output of fluorescence imaging, LP-CDs can visualize the cellular viscosity changes induced by inflammation and hyperglycemia, and image tumor evolution. More importantly, LP-CDs can be used as powerful sensors to screen anticancer drugs in vivo by evaluating therapeutic effects. This work provides a new scheme for constructing robust nanoprobes to achieve the diagnosis and imaging-guided surgery of viscosity related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Viscosity detection
Diseases diagnosis
Therapeutic efficacy assessment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of LP-CDs with other probes for detecting viscosity
In vivo cancer diagnosis with LP-CDs
Therapeutic evaluation and screening of anticancer drugs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (inset: particle size distribution of LP-CDs) and (B) HR TEM image of LP-CDs. (C) The highlighted area of B. (D) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra and (E) FT-IR spectra of LP-CDs. (F) XPS full scan spectrum of LP-CDs, (G) C 1s, (H) N 1s, (I) O 1s HR XPS spectra of LP-CDs.

Photographs of LP-CDs (A) under 365 nm excitation and (B) imaged by PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina imaging system (λex = 480 nm, λem = 610 nm) in PBS/glycerol solution (from left to right, 0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100% Gly). (C) Fluorescence spectra of LP-CDs in different viscosity (λex = 480 nm). (D) Linear relationship between log I610 nm and logη. (E) Quantification of fluorescence intensity in B. (F) Linear relationship between log I670 nm and logη. (G) The fluorescence lifetime spectra of LP-CDs in different viscosity. (H) Linear relationship between logτ and logη. Error bars meant standard derivation (SD), n = 3.

(A) CLSM images of L929 cells (a1-a3), CT26 cells (d1-d3) and B16F10 cells (g1-g3) incubated with LP-CDs. CLSM images of L929 cells (b1-b3), CT26 cells (e1-e3) and B16F10 cells (h1-h3) incubated with LP-CDs and glucose. CLSM images of L929 cells (c1-c3), CT26 cells (f1-f3) and B16F10 cells (i1-i3) incubated with LP-CDs and LPS. Blue channel (Hoechst 33,258,10 μg/mL, λex = 405 nm, λem = 415–510 nm). Green channel (LP-CDs, 150 μg/mL, λex = 488 nm, λem = 490–530 nm). The quantification of fluorescence intensity of (B) a2, d2 and g2, (C) a2, b2 and c2, (D) d2, e2 and f2, (E) g2, h2 and i2. Scale bar: 40 μm. Error bars meant standard derivation (SD), n = 3.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of major organs and tumors (λex = 480 nm, λem = 670 nm). (B) Quantification of fluorescence intensity of the organs and tumor in A. (C) Fluorescence imaging of tumor-bearing mice for different time points (λex = 480 nm, λem = 670 nm). (D) Average fluorescence intensity of tumors (n = 3) in C. (E) Tumor growth curve within 7 d. Error bars meant standard derivation (SD), n = 3.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of LP-CDs in tumor-bearing mice treated or untreated (the control group) with DOX, SAHA, and TAX (λex = 480 nm, λem = 670 nm). (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of LP-CDs at tumor sites in A (n = 3). (C) Tumor growth curves of control group and DOX-, SAHA-, and TAX-treated groups.
(A) Preparation of LP-CDs for probing viscosity. (B) Real-time fluorescence imaging viscosity at cellular and organ levels with LP-CDs.

head.ce:title: Viscosity-activated carbon dots for noninvasive disease diagnosis, therapeutic efficacy assessment and anticancer drug screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal levels of intracellular microviscosity have a close relationship with some diseases and pathologies. Therefore, quantitative imaging of viscosity at the cellular and organ levels is beneficial for disease diagnosis, curative effect evaluation, and anticancer drug screening. Herein, we synthesized viscosity-activated orange-red emitting carbon dots (named as LP-CDs) from lignin and p-phenylenediamine using a one-step method, whose fluorescence intensity, lifetime, and quantum yield significantly increase with rising viscosity. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of LP-CDs also has a good linear relationship with the solution viscosity. Taking the advantages of high sensitivity, noninvasiveness, and rapid result output of fluorescence imaging, LP-CDs can visualize the cellular viscosity changes induced by inflammation and hyperglycemia, and image tumor evolution. More importantly, LP-CDs can be used as powerful sensors to screen anticancer drugs in vivo by evaluating therapeutic effects. This work provides a new scheme for constructing robust nanoprobes to achieve the diagnosis and imaging-guided surgery of viscosity related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: For the microenvironment, intracellular viscosity is an important parameter, which profoundly affects many physiological activities of organisms (Fu et al., 2020; Ni et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020). Previous studies have substantiated that diverse diseases such as inflammation, diabetes and even cancers are usually featured by high viscosity (Guo et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, viscosity is a reliable biomarker for disease diagnosis. On this basis, the development of an instantaneous and non-destructive technique to detect micro-viscosity at cellular and organ levels will be extremely desirable for achieving cancer diagnosis, efficacy evaluation and screening of anticancer drugs. (Fu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023). Fluorescence imaging is a powerful technology for visualizing chemical or biological processes in real time through detectable optical signals. With the advantages of high sensitivity, superb spatial/temporal resolution, simplicity and noninvasiveness, fluorescence technique has become an indispensable tool for sensing intracellular micro-viscosity (Kim et al., 2024; Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2024). Hitherto, a number of organic fluorophores for cellular viscosity sensing have been prepared (Fan et al., 2023; Guan et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024; Zan et al., 2023). For example, Zhu et al. designed a luminescent viscosity sensor for detecting blood viscosity (Zhu et al., 2020). Lin et al. developed a multifunctional optical reagent for monitoring the viscosity in tumor microenvironment (Liang et al., 2021). However, the drawbacks like tedious synthesis process, low water solubility and high cytotoxicity have impeded their biomedical applications. In this regard, exploring a simple way to fabricate viscosity-responsive probes with good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility is urgently needed.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which are composed of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores and plentiful surface functional groups (Liu et al., 2023; Xia et al., 2019). CDs have the outstanding merits of small size (<10 nm), admirable optical performance, benign water solubility, unique biocompatibility, as well as ease of preparation (Wang et al., 2022), which have led to extensive applications in sensing, (Arkin et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2020a,b; Lin et al., 2022), bioimaging (Feng et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022), cancer diagnosis and treatment (Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022, Liu et al., 2023). Particularly, CDs have been employed as superb optical nanosensors for the recognition of biomarkers (e.g. pH (Gao et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2020a,b), glucose (Yu et al., 2022; Xing et al., 2022), lactic acid (Ma et al., 2022; Rossini et al., 2021) and insulin (Abazar et al., 2022; Abazar and Noorbakhsh, 2020) in vitro and in vivo. To date, several CDs have been developed for monitoring intracellular viscosity. For instance, Guo et al. synthesized CDs for detecting mitochondrial viscosity in living cells (Guo et al., 2022). Yan et al. synthesized CDs using citric acid and diethylenetriamine, and then modified the CDs with 2-(2′-hydroxyphenyl)benzothiazole to obtain viscosity-responsive V-CDs (Yan et al., 2021). V-CDs were employed for visually monitoring cancer cell death and inhibiting the production of vascular endothelial growth factor. However, these studies were limited at the cellular level, probably because of the short emission wavelength of CDs. To achieve cancer diagnosis and drug efficacy evaluation in vivo, it is highly desirable to develop viscosity-responsive CDs with long-wavelength emission.

Lignin, derived from vascular plants, ranks as the second largest natural plant substance after cellulose (Li et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2021) comprises numerous benzene rings and an abundance of oxygenated functional groups. Under hydrothermal conditions, the hydroxyl groups of lignin and the amino groups of p-phenylenediamine undergo polymerization, cross-linking and carbonization to produce CDs with a graphitic carbon core and rich surface functional groups (Zhu et al., 2024). Encouraged by these findings, orange-red emissive LP-CDs were prepared (Scheme 1 A), whose fluorescence intensity, lifetime and quantum yield are three dependable indicators for quantitative detection of viscosity. By tracking the fluorescent signals, LP-CDs were capable of not only discriminating normal cells from pathological cells in vitro, but also monitoring tumor growth, appraising therapeutic outcomes and screening anticancer drugs in vivo (Scheme 1B).
See the Experiments Section of the Support Information for details.

LP-CDs were prepared from p-phenylenediamine and alkaline lignin via solvothermal method. In addition, L-CDs or P-CDs were synthesized solely from alkaline lignin or p-phenylenediamine. The morphology and size of LP-CDs were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, LP-CDs have a uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical structure with the size of 4.0–11.0 nm, the average diameter of LP-CDs was about 7.7 ± 0.08 nm. The high-resolution (HR) TEM image showed a lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) crystal plane (Fig. 1B and C). The XRD pattern was shown in Fig. S1, a characteristic peak observed at 2θ = 20° revealed an amorphous carbon phase, ascribing to highly disordered carbon atoms. The structure of LP-CDs was further characterized by Raman spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. S2, the D-band and G-band that corresponding to amorphous carbon and graphitic carbon appeared at 1350 and 1560 cm-1, respectively. Moreover, the ID/IG ratio was 1.31, indicating that LP-CDs contain a large amount of disordered carbon. The zeta-potential of LP-CDs is +18.5 mV. Due to the prevalence of negative charges on the surface of cells, the positive charge of LP-CDs facilitated their cellular uptake. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of LP-CDs was presented in Fig. 1D, which displayed two peaks at 230 and 280 nm, along with a wide absorption band at 500 nm. The former two peaks were mainly caused by the π-π* transition of conjugated aromatic structure in LP-CDs, while the latter band was due to the n-π* transition of CO and C–N. The emission of LP-CDs was excitation-independent in the range of 460–540 nm (Fig. 1D), with the maximum emission at 610 nm under 480 nm excitation. By contrast, the maximum emission wavelength of L-CDs (Fig. S3) and P-CDs (Fig. S4) was 537 nm and 400 nm, respectively, under the excitation of 378 and 320 nm. The surface functional groups of LP-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Fig. 1E). The stretching vibrations at 3050-3650 cm−1 were derived from O–H and N–H. The characteristic peaks at 2680-2960 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The peaks corresponding to the functional groups, such as CO (1720 cm−1), CC (1630 cm−1), N–H (1510 cm−1), C–N (1380 cm−1), C–O–C (1260 cm−1) and C–O (1120 cm−1) were also observed. The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) full scan spectrum of LP-CDs (Fig. 1F) exhibited three peaks at 285 (C 1s), 400 (N 1s), and 532 (O 1s) eV, indicating that LP-CDs primarily consisted of C, N, and O elements. The C, N, and O content was calculated to 76.83%, 3.25%, and 19.92%, respectively. In HR XPS analysis, C 1s could be decomposed into three peaks at 284.7 (C–C/CC), 285.5 (C–O), and 288.6 (CO) eV (Fig. 1G). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1H) could be decomposed into two peaks: pyridine N (399.2 eV) and pyrrole N (400.1 eV). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1I) confirmed the existence of C–O (532.2 eV) and CO (531.5 eV).

The fluorescence response of LP-CDs to viscosity was investigated by different ratio of Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS, pH = 7.4)/glycerol (Gly) solution. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the emission intensity of LP-CDs exhibited a gradual increment as the viscosity of the solution rose, and the fluorescence changed from almost colorless to bright orange. The solution was imaged by PerkinElmer IVIS spectral imaging system under 480 nm excitation and 670 nm emission (Fig. 2B), and the quantitative results of fluorescence intensity were shown in Fig. 2E. With the increase of viscosity, intense red emission could be observed. The emission response of LP-CDs to viscosity (η, 1–939 cP) was measured (Fig. 2C). The viscosity values of different ratios of PBS (pH = 7.4)/glycerol solutions were determined using a Ubbelohde viscometer and tabulated in Table S1. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a gradual rise in correlation with the progressive elevation of solution viscosity. As the viscosity increased from 1.0 to 939.0 cp, the fluorescence intensity increased by nearly 7-fold. In addition, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and viscosity could be quantitatively expressed by Förster-Hoffmann linear equation: log (I610) = 0.24logη + 1.54 (Fig. 2D), the detection limit of LP-CDs for viscosity was 3.0 cp. The results in Fig. 2E demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity increased by about 12-fold with the elevated viscosity of the solution. A linear correlation between log I670 and logη could be determined by utilizing Förster-Hoffmann equation, log (I670) = 0.35logη + 7.78 (Fig. 2F), and the detection limit of LP-CDs for viscosity was determined to be 4.03 cP. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of LP-CDs in different viscosity was also evaluated (Fig. 2G) and listed in Table S2. The average lifetime in 1.0, 3.5, 16.5, 100.0, 180.0 and 939.0 cp was 3.05, 3.23, 4.27, 4.88, 4.92 and 5.80 ns, respectively (Table S2). The good linear relationship between logτ and logη, was quantitatively expressed by Förster-Hoffmann linear equation: logτ = 0.01logη +0.89 (Fig. 2H). Meanwhile, we also tested the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of LP-CDs in different viscosity. As displayed in Table S2, the Φ in η = 1.05, 3.5, 16.5, 100.0, 180.0 and 939.0 solution was 1.71%, 2.81%, 2.92%, 4.65%, 5.62%, and 8.45%, respectively. The above results proved that the fluorescence intensity, lifetime, as well as Φ of LP-CDs could serve as reliable indicators for the quantitative detection of viscosity. To investigate the causes of the fluorescence response of LP-CDs to viscosity, the fluorescence spectra of L-CDs and P-CDs in viscosity were also examined. As depicted in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs increased with rising viscosity. However, there was no linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and viscosity. As for P-CDs (Fig. S6), the fluorescence intensity fluctuated randomly with viscosity. During the carbonization of lignin to form LP-CDs, numerous functional groups remained on the surface. As the viscosity increased, the molecular rotation of LP-CDs was restricted, which weakened the non-radiative transition of photo-excited electrons and enhanced the radiative transition, resulting in the enhancement of fluorescence.

The LP-CDs solution in different ratio of PBS (pH = 7.4)/glycerol (0%, 50%, and 100% glycerol) was irradiated by a green lamp (18 mW/cm−2) for 180 min, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra were monitored. As displayed in Fig. S7, all the emission intensity remained almost unchanged, indicating that LP-CDs had excellent photostability.

To ascertain that the fluorescence intensity of LP-CDs was sensitive to viscosity rather than polarity, the fluorescence spectra of LP-CDs in various polar solvents were measured. As depicted in Fig. S8, there was no discernible trend of change in fluorescence intensity with solvent polarity. The fluorescence intensity in different concentration of NaCl solution did not change significantly (Fig. S9), which verified the outstanding anti-ion interference ability of LP-CDs. Moreover, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of LP-CDs was also investigated. The various pH solutions were prepared utilizing citric acid-sodium citrate buffer solution, with the preparation methodology outlined in Table S3. It was worth noting that the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged in the pH range of 2–8 (Fig. S10), indicating that the detection of viscosity by LP-CDs was not susceptible to the pH of the intracellular environment. Besides, the effects of 38 interferents on the emission of LP-CDs were investigated (Fig. S11). Anions, cations, amino acids, ATP, VC, active oxygen, reactive nitrogen species, and active sulfur did not obviously affect the emission of LP-CDs, demonstrating that the interferents in physiological media would not affect the response of LP-CDs to viscosity. The above results verified that LP-CDs could act as promising viscosity probes for practical biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of LP-CDs with other probes for detecting viscosity
ce:section.ce:para: LP-CDs was compared with existing fluorescent probes in terms of excitation wavelength and emission wavelength, water solubility, detection limit and application. Table S4 showed that LP-CDs has the advantages of good water solubility and wide application.

The microculture tetrazolium assay (MTT) assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of LP-CDs before conducting in vitro imaging. Fig. S12 demonstrated that when L929, B16F10, and CT26 cells were exposed to varying concentration of LP-CDs for 24 h, more than 80% of the cells remained viable, indicating that LP-CDs have low toxicity and were suitable for monitoring viscosity in complex biological environment.

Given the outstanding properties of LP-CDs, their applications in monitoring intracellular viscosity variation by fluorescence imaging were further investigated. The onset of certain diseases is frequently accompanied by an abnormal elevation of intracellular viscosity. To establish pathological cell models, exogenous drugs (glucose and LPS) were utilized to stimulate cells into hyperglycemic and inflammatory states, respectively, thereby leading to elevated viscosity of cells. Normal cells L929, cancerous cells CT26 and B16F10 were stimulated with glucose or LPS (150 μg/mL), respectively. The unstimulated cells were set as the control groups. Following 30 min of incubation with LP-CDs (150 μg/mL), the cells were observed under the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). As presented in Fig. 3a1-a3, 3d1-d3 and 3g1-g3, the emission of the three control groups was relatively weak in the absence of glucose or LPS. However, the fluorescence intensity of CT26 and B16F10 cells was 1.38- and 1.13 times higher than that of L929 cells, respectively, which was attributed to the increased viscosity caused by the vigorous metabolism of cancer cells. Under the stimulus of glucose (Fig. 3b1 -b3, 3e1-4e3, and 3h1-4h3) or LPS (Fig. 3c1-c3, 3f1-f3, and 3i1-i3), the experimental groups exhibited significantly higher fluorescence intensity, compared to the control groups, proving that hyperglycemia or inflammation occurred in the experimental groups. The quantification of fluorescence intensity was shown in Fig. 3B–E. Compared to the control groups, the glucose-stimulated groups exhibited a 1.36-, 1.21-, and 1.37-fold increase in emission, respectively, while the LPS-stimulated groups showed a 1.39-, 1.44-, and 1.63-fold increase, respectively. These results demonstrated that LP-CDs have superior capability of diagnosing viscosity-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo cancer diagnosis with LP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by LP-CDs’ excellent capability of imaging intracellular viscosity, attention turned to exploring the applications of LP-CDs in monitoring the viscosity in organs and tumors. 4T1 tumor-bearing Balb/c mice model was established, and the mice were euthanized once the tumor diameter reached 8–12 mm. The major organs and tumors were incubated with LP-CDs (250 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 4 h, followed by a rinse with PBS, and then imaged by PerkinElmer IVIS Lumina imaging system. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, only the tumor tissue emitted intense fluorescence signal due to the increased viscosity. By contrast, the fluorescence in major organs was very weak. Therefore, LP-CDs could readily discriminate tumors from normal organs by comparing emission intensity.

Further in vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted to explore whether LP-CDs could track physiological viscosity variations in the course of cancer evolution. On the first day (1 d) when the 4T1 tumor size reached 8–12 mm, LP-CDs were injected into the tumor site, and in vivo fluorescence imaging was carried out 5 min later. Thereafter, LP-CDs were administrated via intratumoral injection every other day (3 d, 5 d and 7 d) and bioimaging was conducted in the similar way. As displayed in Fig. 4C, no signal appeared in the control group due to the absence of LP-CDs. In contrast, after the administration of LP-CDs, the fluorescence signal emerged in the tumor sites, and continued to strengthen with tumor growth. The quantification of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4D) demonstrated that compared to that of 1 d, the signal on 3, 5 and 7 d was enhanced by 1.8-, 3.3-, and 3.6-fold, respectively. The tumor growth curve (Fig. 4E) showed a steady increase in tumor volume over 7 d. Simultaneously, the weight of the mice presented steady growth over this period (Fig. S13), suggesting a good biosafety of LP-CDs. These results well substantiated that LP-CDs could be used as a high-contrast visualization tool to monitor tumor evolution in real time by tracing viscosity fluctuations in the tumor microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic evaluation and screening of anticancer drugs
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic evaluation and screening of anticancer drugs are very important for raising cancer survival rate. In this regard, the feasibility of using LP-CDs to assess the therapeutic outcomes of anticancer drugs was investigated. When the 4T1 tumors grew to about 8–12 mm in diameter, three anticancer drugs doxorubicin (DOX), vorinostat (SAHA) and paclitaxel (TAX) were administered via tail vein injection, once every other day, respectively. The untreated tumor-bearing mice were set as the control group. On 1, 3, 5 and 7 d, the mice were subjected to in vivo fluorescence imaging 5 min after intratumoral injection of LP-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 5 A and B, the signal in the control group strikingly enhanced over time, with the intensity on 7 d being 1.86 times higher than that of day 1, indicating continued tumor growth. On the contrary, the emission signals of DOX-, SAHA- and TAX-treated groups were attenuated to varying degrees. After 7 d of treatment, the signal in the DOX, SAHA and TAX groups was attenuated by 14%, 31% and 42%, respectively, verifying that TAX had the strongest anti-tumor therapeutic outcomes. In addition, the tumor volume was measured, as shown in Fig. 5C. In comparison with continued tumor growth in the control group, the tumor volume in drug-treated groups (especially the TAX group) was significantly reduced. The result of tumor growth curves was extremely consistent with that obtained from in vivo fluorescence imaging. Therefore, by tracking the viscosity changes in tumor microenvironment, LP-CDs could not only appraise the efficacy of drugs, but also successfully accomplish the screening of anticancer drugs.

Unprecedented CDs-based viscosity-responsive optical nanosensors with excellent photostability and favorable biocompatibility were constructed, whose fluorescence intensity, lifetime and quantum yield exhibit high sensitivity, superior selectivity and fast response to viscosity variations. With the aid of fluorescence imaging technology, LP-CDs can distinguish between normal cells and pathological cells, enabling real-time on-line monitoring of tumor evolution. Furthermore, this unique viscosity-sensitive fluorescence probe has been successfully applied for efficacy evaluation and screening of anticancer drugs in vivo. Based on the above discussion, it is envisioned that LP-CDs can serve as a reliable and durable device for viscosity-related pathological and pharmacological studies. Unfortunately, the mechanism of LP-CDs response to viscosity requires further investigation. Therefore, the primary focus of our laboratory's upcoming work will be to delve into the mechanism of LP-CDs' response to viscosity.

Mingxin Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zhigang Xie: Supervision, Resources. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Science and Technology Development Plan of Changchun City (No. 21ZY38).
